countries.d1Html:
🚡

Day 1 — Kok-Tobe Cable Car + Green Bazaar

🚡 Kok-Tobe — The Hill Above Almaty

📍 Kok-Tobe Hill, Almaty⏱ 2–3 hours💰 ~₹800 (family)

Kok-Tobe (Blue Hill) rises 1,100m above Almaty. The cable car glides above the treetops with the city below and the snow-capped Tian Shan mountains immediately behind — a mountain range that extends 2,500 km from China to Kyrgyzstan. At the top: a small amusement park, a zoo with deer and peacocks, restaurants, and the famous Beatles statue (a quirky Soviet-era tribute).

Rides for children — small rollercoaster, train ride, bumper cars. She'll play here for an hour.
The view of the Tian Shan from the top is extraordinary — an unbroken wall of 4,000m snow-capped peaks directly behind the modern city
The cable car journey itself is beautiful — 4 minutes each way, floating above the forest with mountain views
countries.d2Html:
⛸

Day 2 — Medeu + Shymbulak

⭐ Medeu — World's Highest Ice Rink

📍 Medeu Valley, 1,691m altitude, 15 km from Almaty⏱ 2 hours skating💰 ~₹700 (family, entry + skates)

Medeu is the world's highest ice-skating rink — an Olympic-size outdoor rink at 1,691m altitude, surrounded by the snow-capped peaks of the Tian Shan mountains. The scenery from the rink is extraordinary — she skates with mountain peaks rising above her on all sides.

Skate rental available at the rink — no need to bring anything. Instructors available for beginners at additional cost (~₹500/30 min)
Even if she can't skate, the rink is beautiful to watch from the viewing area, and the 842 steps to the rink are worth climbing for the mountain views
In summer (June–August), the rink is sometimes closed — check in advance. The Shymbulak gondola above Medeu is always open in summer for alpine hiking.
countries.d3Html:
🏔

Day 3 — Charyn Canyon + Big Almaty Lake

⭐ Charyn Canyon — Kazakhstan's Grand Canyon

📍 200 km east of Almaty⏱ Full morning💰 ~₹3,900 (family, car + entry)

The Charyn Canyon is 90 km long and up to 300m deep — carved by the Charyn River over 3 million years. The Valley of Castles section has towering rust-red sandstone formations that genuinely look like an alien landscape. Smaller than the American Grand Canyon but equally dramatic in its own way.

The 3 km walk through the Valley of Castles is completely flat — perfectly safe and manageable for a 6-year-old
The ochre and rust-red rock colours are extraordinary at any time of day — especially beautiful in morning and late afternoon light
In spring (May–June), the canyon floor has wildflowers among the red rock — spectacular colour contrast

⭐ Big Almaty Lake

📍 2,511m altitude, 35 km from Almaty⏱ 1 hour visit

An extraordinary turquoise-emerald alpine lake at 2,511m altitude, surrounded by 4,000m peaks on three sides. The colour comes from glacial rock flour suspended in the meltwater — it changes from turquoise to emerald depending on the sunlight. One of the most beautiful lakes in all of Central Asia.

The access road is bumpy 4WD territory — hire a local 4WD taxi from the canyon (drivers know the route)
Best viewed from the dam at the lake outlet — the full lake and surrounding peaks are visible from this single viewpoint
countries.d4Html:
🏛

Day 4 — Central State Museum + Departure

🏛 The Golden Man — Kazakhstan's Most Famous Treasure

The Central State Museum's most famous exhibit is the Issyk Golden Man — a reconstruction of a 2,400-year-old Scythian warrior found in a kurgan (burial mound) near Almaty in 1969, wearing a complete suit of armour covered in 4,000 golden ornaments. Your daughter will stand open-mouthed in front of this glittering figure from the ancient steppes.

The Kazakh yurt exhibit — a full-sized traditional nomadic yurt with all its furnishings reconstructed. She can sit inside a real Kazakh home from the steppes.
Best Kazakh souvenirs: Dried apricots and walnuts from Green Bazaar (₹400–800), traditional felt nomad crafts (₹500–1,500), Kazakh silver jewellery (₹800–3,000), locally produced honey from the Tian Shan mountains (₹300–600)

countries.overviewHtml:
📋

4-Day Itinerary — Tashkent + Samarkand

🌿Uzbekistan is a secular state — no dress codes, no religious obligations for tourists. The great monuments of Samarkand and Bukhara are visited purely as extraordinary historical and architectural experiences. They are among the most magnificent buildings constructed anywhere on earth.
💰Uzbekistan is the cheapest international destination from Hyderabad. Flights from ₹36,000 (3 pax return). Hotel: ₹2,000–5,000/night. A full restaurant meal for the family: ₹300–500. The Registan is free entry for children. Total 4-day trip budget can be as low as ₹65,000 for 3 people.

countries.d1Html:
🏛

Day 1 — Tashkent + Chorsu Bazaar

🏛 Chorsu Bazaar — Central Asia's Greatest Market

📍 Old City, Tashkent⏱ 2 hours🎟 Free entry

Chorsu Bazaar is one of the oldest bazaars in Central Asia — a vast covered market under a blue Soviet-era dome, surrounded by open-air stalls. The scale, colour, and smell of the spice section alone is overwhelming. It is a completely secular, family-friendly shopping and cultural experience.

The spice section — 50+ varieties of spice in enormous open sacks, colours from bright orange turmeric to deep red sumac to purple-black Tashkent pepper
Dried fruit stalls — towers of Uzbek dried apricots, mulberries, figs, and raisins. She can taste everything — vendors expect it and are happy to give samples
Bread section — enormous golden discs of non flatbread baked in tandoor ovens right in the market. Buy one for ₹25 and eat it fresh — the best bread in Central Asia
💡Also visit: Tashkent Metro on Day 1 evening (₹30/person) — the stations are Soviet-era masterpieces, each different and extraordinarily ornate. The Kosmonavtlar station (carved spaceship reliefs) will amaze your daughter. One of the most unexpected experiences in all of Central Asia.
countries.d2Html:
🕌

Day 2 — Registan (The World's Most Beautiful Square)

⭐ Registan Square — Samarkand

📍 Registan Square, Samarkand (2 hrs from Tashkent by high-speed train)⏱ 2–3 hours🎟 ~₹2,000 (2 adults · children FREE)

The Registan is widely considered the most beautiful square in the world — three 15th–17th century madrasahs (academic buildings) completely covered in turquoise, cobalt-blue, and gold mosaic tilework, facing each other across a vast plaza. Commissioned by Timur (Tamerlane), the conqueror of half the known world, it rivals the Taj Mahal in sheer visual impact.

Stand in the centre of the plaza at dawn (7–8 AM before crowds) — the three tilework facades glow in the early morning light. The scale is overwhelming.
Children under 12 are FREE at the Registan — only adults pay. A huge saving.
The tile patterns are purely geometric — no figurative imagery. The mathematical precision of the 15th-century craftsmen is astounding.
The lion-and-sun motifs on the Sher-Dor Madrasah (added in the 17th century) are the most photographed detail — she'll spot the lions immediately
🚅The Afrosiyob high-speed train (250 km/h, 2 hours) from Tashkent to Samarkand is an essential part of the Uzbekistan experience. Book tickets at uzbekrailways.uz — prices increase as departure approaches. Buy tickets as soon as you book flights.
countries.d3Html:
🎨

Day 3 — Ceramic Workshop + Tashkent Metro

⭐ Uzbek Ceramic Painting Workshop

📍 Old City craft studios, Tashkent or Samarkand⏱ 1.5 hours💰 ~₹1,500 (family incl. materials)

Uzbek ceramic painting is one of the world's great craft traditions — the same turquoise-and-cobalt geometric patterns that decorate the Registan, applied to hand-turned pottery. A master craftsman teaches the traditional technique. Your daughter paints her own ceramic plate to take home.

The geometric patterns are easier for children than figurative art — she can successfully paint a beautiful plate with guidance
The fired plate is ready in a few hours — arrange pickup before departure or have it shipped to Hyderabad (wrap carefully in clothes in your suitcase)

🚇 Tashkent Metro — The Most Beautiful Underground in Asia

The Tashkent Metro (opened 1977) was built as a showpiece of Soviet Central Asia — each station was designed by a different architect with a different theme, using marble, bronze, ceramic tile, and chandeliers. Riding the entire Chilonzor line is like visiting 7 different palaces underground.

Kosmonavtlar Station — space exploration theme, with carved reliefs of early cosmonauts and planetary systems covering the walls. Your daughter will be amazed by the spacecraft carvings.
Alisher Navoi Station — ornate Uzbek classical architecture underground, with hand-painted ceramic panels depicting scenes from Uzbek epic poetry
Cost: ₹30 per person per journey — the cheapest museum in the world
countries.d4Html:
🛍

Day 4 — Silk Road Shopping + Departure

🛍 Best Uzbekistan Souvenirs

Dried Uzbek apricots and mulberries — Uzbekistan grows the world's finest dried apricots. The Khurmati (sour dried apricots) are extraordinary. Buy 1–2 kg at Chorsu Bazaar (₹200–400/kg). They pack beautifully and keep for months.
Suzani embroidery — traditional large-scale embroidered wall hangings in bold floral patterns made on handlooms. A genuine handmade suzani is a beautiful home decoration (₹3,000–15,000 depending on size).
Ikat silk fabric — Uzbekistan's most famous textile. Vibrant silk in traditional geometric patterns. Buy a length of fabric (₹800–2,500) for a scarf or traditional outfit.
Hand-painted ceramics — turquoise-and-cobalt plates, bowls, and tiles in traditional Uzbek patterns (₹400–1,500). Perfect for every relative.
Samarkand paper — hand-made paper using the ancient method, sold as notebooks, booklets, or loose sheets. An extraordinary souvenir from the birthplace of Central Asian papermaking (₹200–600).
